name: Pipestone Lamb Stew
recipeIngredient: 1 large Bunch fresh thyme sprigs or
4 lb Lamb stew meat with bones, trimmed
2 medium Onions, peeled and halved; cut into 1/4 inch thick slices
6 Large Garlic cloves, peeled; coarsely chopped
28 oz Can plum tomatoes (crush with hands or wooden spoon)
3 medium Carrots, peeled and cut into 1/2 inch thick slices
8 oz Button mushrooms, stems trimmed and caps coarsely chopped
2 medium Waxy Potatoes, peeled and quartered
2 medium Russet Potatoes, peeled and cut into eighths
recipeInstructions:
--------------------------
1. Sm Garnish: 1 bunch Italian parsley (optional).
Strip leaves from thyme sprigs; measure about 2 tablespoons of fresh thyme.
Cut carrots diagonally into 1/2 inch thick slices.
Cut celery ribs diagonally into 1/2 inch thick slices.
Tie bay leaves, thyme, and allspice berries together in a piece of cheesecloth; set aside.
Brown lamb on all sides in a large (at least 8 quart) heavy stockpot over medium-high heat, about 8 to 10 minutes. Add onions and garlic and cook, stirring frequently, until onions begin to turn translucent, about 3 to 4 minutes. Add the cheesecloth bundle, wine, horseradish, Worcestershire sauce, and all vegetables. Season generously with salt and pepper. Bring mixture to a boil, then cover, reduce heat to medium, and cook until potatoes are tender and lamb is falling from the bone, about 3 hours. (Alternatively, transfer stew to a preheated 350°F oven and cook for the same amount of time.)
Remove lid and continue cooking the stew until it has thickened slightly but remains soupy, an additional 1 to 1 1/2 hours. Remove cheesecloth bundle and season to taste; add more horseradish if desired. Mince parsley and sprinkle over stew. Serve immediately.
Yield: 8 to 10 servings. St. Francis Merlot 1987 pairs well with this dish.
Note: This is a great dish to prepare ahead. Remove cheesecloth bundle and refrigerate stew overnight. The next day, skim any solidified fat from the surface and reheat stew. Season to taste; add more horseradish if desired. Garnish with parsley and serve.
Loomis writes: "This recipe comes from Ann Olson, whose husband, Sherman, raises sheep on their farm in Danvers, Minnesota. Ann makes various lamb dishes throughout the year, and this is one of her favorites ('Pipestone' refers to a town in southern Minnesota where youngsters learn to raise sheep). Horseradish adds a pleasant bite to lamb stew meat, often neck meat, which has some of the most flavorful parts of the animal. If using neck meat, consider removing bones before serving.")
From Farm House Cookbook by Susan Herrmann Loomis. New York: Workman Publishing Company, Inc., 1991. Pp. 135-136. ISBN 0-89480-772-2.
